# Maillard Reaction

The Maillard reaction is a chemical reaction between amino acids and reducing
sugars that gives browned food its distinctive flavour. It begins around 140 °C
and is distinct from caramelisation, which involves only sugars. The reaction
produces hundreds of aroma compounds, which is why a seared steak, toasted bread
and roasted coffee smell so different despite sharing the same underlying process.

## Why dry heat matters

Because the reaction needs high, dry heat, it explains why boiled meat stays grey
while pan-seared meat turns brown and savoury: surface water caps the temperature
at 100 °C, below the browning threshold. Cooks raise browning by drying the
surface, adding a pinch of sugar, or brushing with milk or egg, which supply extra
reactants for the reaction to consume.
